does: This paper develops a principled evaluation framework for sparse autoencoders (SAEs) and other unsupervised dictionary learning methods by comparing them against supervised feature dictionaries as ground truth. The authors construct supervised dictionaries that explicitly capture task-relevant features for the indirect object identification task in GPT-2 Small, then measure how well these supervised dictionaries perform on three axes: approximation quality, model control (ability to manipulate model behavior), and interpretability. They then apply these same metrics to evaluate unsupervised SAEs trained on either the IOI task or OpenWebText data. The key finding is that while unsupervised SAEs recover interpretable features, they substantially underperform supervised dictionaries at controlling model computations, revealing a critical gap between current unsupervised approaches and task-specific feature discovery.
connects: This work addresses a fundamental challenge in mechanistic interpretability: validating that discovered features are genuinely meaningful without access to ground truth, a problem that has plagued recent sparse dictionary learning and SAE research. It builds on prior work using supervised probing and linear classifiers to extract task-relevant features, extending these ideas from simple feature extraction to comprehensive dictionary evaluation. The paper responds to growing interest in SAEs as an unsupervised alternative to hand-crafted or supervised interpretability methods, while grounding evaluation in the well-studied IOI task that has become a benchmark for interpretability research. It combines classical dictionary learning theory with modern mechanistic interpretability concerns around feature quality, control, and faithful representation of model internals.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more rigorous evaluation of future interpretability methods beyond SAEs, including other unsupervised feature discovery techniques. By establishing supervised dictionaries as a principled benchmark, the work enables researchers to quantify progress toward faithful, controllable feature representations in language models. The three-axis evaluation (approximation, control, interpretability) provides a reusable methodology that could be applied to other architectures and tasks, moving the field toward standardized evaluation practices. Additionally, identifying the control gap between supervised and unsupervised features suggests specific research directions: improving SAE training objectives to better capture task-relevant structure, developing hybrid approaches that incorporate task information into unsupervised learning, or creating better architectures for feature discovery in neural networks.
